title: The IMF, World Bank & global policy
summary:
## The big idea When a country runs out of foreign currency, who does *it* borrow from? When the poorest countries need dams, schools and vaccination programmes no market will finance, who steps in? In 1944, with the world economy in ruins, the **Bretton Woods** conference built the machinery: the **IMF** for financial emergencies, the **World Bank** for long-term development — joined later by the **WTO** for trade rules. Between them they form the international layer of macro policy, and one of the most evaluative, synoptic topics in the course. ## The International Monetary Fund (IMF) **Mission: global monetary and financial stability.** Three jobs: - **Surveillance** — monitoring members' economies and warning of trouble (annual 'Article IV' health checks). - **Lending** — **short-to-medium-term loans to countries in balance-of-payments or currency crises**: the nearest thing nations have to a lender of last resort. When reserves run dry and no private lender will touch a country, the IMF's seal of approval is often what re-opens the markets. - **Technical assistance** — helping build central banks, tax systems, statistics. **Conditionality — the controversial part.** IMF money arrives with strings: typically **fiscal austerity** (cut deficits — often via subsidy cuts and public-sector restraint), **monetary tightening**, **devaluation or floating** of an overvalued currency, and **structural reforms** (liberalise, privatise, deregulate). The logic: the crisis came from living beyond the country's means or a distorted economy, and lenders need assurance of repayment. **The critique**, sharpened by decades of experience: austerity in a slump **deepens the recession** (the Keynesian objection); subsidy cuts and devaluation hit **the poorest** first (bread and fuel prices); one-size-fits-all prescriptions ignore local institutions; conditionality overrides **democratic sovereignty**; and repeated rescues create **moral hazard** — governments and their creditors behave badly, expecting rescue. The IMF has partly adapted: more attention to social safety nets and poverty floors in programme design. ## The World Bank **Mission: long-term development and poverty reduction.** Where the IMF fights fires, the Bank builds houses: - **Long-term loans and grants** — at below-market rates, with its IDA arm lending nearly interest-free to the poorest countries — for **infrastructure** (power, transport, water), **human capital** (education, health) and institutional reform. - Knowledge, data and evaluation for development policy (its poverty lines and development indicators are the world's reference numbers). **Critiques:** grand projects that failed or displaced communities (dams are the classic case); debt burdens from loans for projects that never paid off; a history of attaching Washington-Consensus conditions much like the IMF's; and governance dominated by rich countries (by convention, an American leads the Bank, a European the IMF — a standing grievance of the Global South). ## The WTO — the third pillar Covered with protectionism: rules-based trade liberalisation and **dispute settlement**. Its strength is the rulebook; its weakness is stalled negotiation (Doha) and the drift toward regional deals. ## Structural adjustment and the development debate **Structural adjustment programmes (SAPs)** — the 1980s–90s packages tying IMF/Bank money to liberalisation — are the topic's great case study. Supporters: they ended hyperinflations, closed unsustainable deficits and forced overdue reform. Critics: across Africa and Latin America, health and education spending fell, poverty rose short-run, and growth often disappointed — stability was bought, transformation wasn't. The modern consensus stresses **institutions and governance**, country ownership of programmes, and targeted poverty reduction — the framing now organised around the **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**. ## Case files (learn two or three) - **Greece (2010–18):** the IMF joined EU institutions in giant bailouts conditioned on severe austerity. Solvency was restored — at the cost of a ~25% GDP contraction and mass unemployment; the IMF itself later conceded the multipliers were underestimated. The twist: inside the euro, Greece couldn't devalue — all adjustment fell on wages and spending ('internal devaluation'). - **Argentina:** serial programmes, including the Fund's largest-ever (2018) — and yet recurring crises: exhibit A for both moral hazard and the limits of conditionality without domestic ownership. - **Egypt (2016– ):** loans conditioned on floating the pound, subsidy reform and fiscal consolidation — reserves and growth recovered while inflation squeezed real incomes: a textbook conditionality trade-off (and the exchange-rate analysis of Unit 9 in action). - **Ghana, Pakistan, Sri Lanka:** further variations — commodity dependence, debt distress, and the modern complication of owing China as well as the Bretton Woods twins. ## Why it matters This topic is where everything meets: exchange rates and the balance of payments (Unit 9/AS 4), fiscal and monetary policy (Unit 5), development (Unit 9), government failure (Units 3/8). Exam formula: **know each institution's distinct role, explain conditionality with a case, and weigh stability gained against austerity's costs** — reaching a judgement that respects both.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Bretton Woods (1944)** → **IMF** (short-term BoP/currency crisis lending + surveillance) and **World Bank** (long-term development loans/grants); **WTO** = trade rules + disputes. - **Conditionality**: austerity, tight money, devaluation/float, liberalisation/privatisation — assurance for lenders. - Critiques: austerity deepens slumps, hits the poor, one-size-fits-all, sovereignty loss, **moral hazard**. - **SAPs**: stabilised prices and deficits, but social spending and short-run poverty suffered → modern shift to institutions, ownership, **SDGs**. - Cases: **Greece** (austerity in a currency union), **Argentina** (serial bailouts), **Egypt 2016** (float + subsidy reform), plus Ghana/Pakistan/Sri Lanka. - Exam frame: IMF ≠ World Bank; conditionality trade-off (stability vs hardship); synoptic links to exchange rates, fiscal policy, development.

keyTerms.definition: An institution promoting global financial stability and lending short-term to countries in crisis.
An institution providing long-term loans and grants to promote development and reduce poverty.
The body that promotes and regulates rules-based free trade and settles trade disputes.
Policy conditions (e.g. austerity, reform) attached to IMF/World Bank loans.
Loan conditions requiring market liberalisation, fiscal discipline and reform.
The 1944 conference that established the IMF and World Bank.
Policies to cut a government's budget deficit through lower spending and/or higher taxes.

diagrams: No specific diagram is required. Support answers with **AD/AS** (austerity shifting AD left), the **FOREX/exchange-rate** diagram (devaluation) and **current-account** analysis from Unit 9.
tips: Distinguish the **IMF (short-term stability & BoP crises)** from the **World Bank (long-term development)** — a common exam distinction.
Explain **conditionality/SAPs** and give a **balanced evaluation** (stability & confidence vs austerity, poverty and lost sovereignty).
Link IMF programmes to **exchange rates, the current account and inflation** from Unit 9 for synoptic marks.
Use real IMF cases (**Greece's austerity, Argentina's repeated programmes, or Ghana/Pakistan/Egypt reforms**) as powerful application/evaluation examples.
Mention modern shifts toward **institutions, governance and the SDGs** for up-to-date evaluation.

keyConcepts.title: The IMF and World Bank explained

revisionNotes:
# IMF, World Bank & global policy — quick revision

**The institutions (don't mix them up)**
- **IMF** — monetary/financial **stability**: surveillance, **short-term crisis lending** (BoP/currency), technical help. Lender of last resort for countries.
- **World Bank** — **long-term development**: cheap loans/grants (IDA for poorest) for infrastructure, education, health; data & research.
- **WTO** — rules-based trade + dispute settlement.
- All trace to **Bretton Woods, 1944** (WTO via GATT).

**Conditionality (IMF programmes)**
- Typical package: fiscal **austerity** (subsidy cuts), monetary tightening, **devaluation/float**, liberalisation/privatisation (SAPs).
- For: restores credibility & reserves, re-opens markets, forces overdue reform.
- Against: pro-cyclical austerity deepens recession; poorest hit (bread/fuel); one-size-fits-all; sovereignty; **moral hazard**.
- Modern shift: social floors, country ownership, institutions/governance, **SDGs**.

**Cases**
- **Greece 2010–18**: austerity inside the euro (no devaluation) → deep depression; IMF admitted multiplier errors.
- **Argentina**: largest-ever programme (2018), repeated crises — conditionality's limits.
- **Egypt 2016–**: float + subsidy reform → stability regained, inflation pain (Unit 9 links).
- Ghana/Pakistan/Sri Lanka: debt distress + China as parallel creditor.

**Exam frame** — role distinction + conditionality trade-off (stability vs hardship) + one developed case + synoptic links (exchange rate, fiscal policy, development).
questions:
# Practice questions — The IMF, World Bank & global policy **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** The primary role of the IMF is to: - A — provide long-term finance for development projects - B — lend to countries facing balance-of-payments and currency crises and oversee monetary stability - C — settle trade disputes between members - D — set world interest rates
▸ Show answer

B. Short-term crisis lending plus surveillance is the IMF's job. A describes the World Bank; C the WTO.

**2.** 'Conditionality' refers to: - A — the interest rate charged on IMF loans - B — the policy requirements a borrowing country must implement to receive IMF funds - C — the IMF's voting rules - D — a country's credit rating
▸ Show answer

B. Loans are disbursed in tranches against agreed measures — typically austerity, monetary tightening, exchange-rate adjustment and structural reform.

**3.** Which is the best example of moral hazard arising from IMF lending? - A — a government undertakes painful reforms to avoid ever needing the IMF - B — private lenders extend risky loans to a country, expecting an IMF rescue if it defaults - C — the IMF refuses to lend to a country in crisis - D — a country repays its IMF loan early
▸ Show answer

B. The safety net changes behaviour: creditors (and governments) take risks whose downside they expect the rescue to absorb.

**4.** A country receives a 30-year, near-zero-interest loan to build rural schools and clinics. The lender is most likely: - A — the IMF - B — the World Bank (IDA) - C — the WTO - D — a commercial bank
▸ Show answer

B. Long-term, concessional development finance for human capital is the World Bank's terrain — its IDA arm serves the poorest countries on the softest terms.

## Paper 4 style — data response > A tourism-dependent economy loses access to bond markets after its debt reaches 110% of GDP and reserves fall to three weeks of imports. It agrees a \$5 billion IMF programme conditioned on: floating the overvalued currency; halving fuel subsidies; a public-sector wage freeze; and privatising two state enterprises. One year on: the currency has fallen 35%; inflation is 18%; reserves cover four months of imports; bond markets have re-opened at high but falling yields; and street protests against fuel prices continue. The World Bank has separately lent \$800 million for a cash-transfer scheme protecting the poorest 30% of households. **(a)** Distinguish between the roles of the IMF and the World Bank as shown in the passage. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why the IMF required the currency to float. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse the short-run economic and social effects of the conditionality package. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the programme has served the country well. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) IMF: crisis lending with policy conditions to restore external stability. World Bank: longer-term development/poverty lending (the cash-transfer scheme). [1+1]

(b) The peg was overvalued: defending it drained reserves (three weeks left) and kept exports/tourism uncompetitive. Floating restores the adjustment mechanism (depreciation → competitiveness) and stops reserve bleed — conditions for repayment. [4]

(c) Economic: depreciation + subsidy cuts → 18% inflation (imported + administered prices); wage freeze → falling real incomes → AD squeeze; but reserves rebuilt, market access returning. Social: fuel poverty and protests — costs fall disproportionately on the poor, partly offset by the targeted transfers. [6]

(d) For: default avoided, reserves quadrupled, credibility returning (falling yields), safety net attached — the counterfactual (disorderly default) was worse. Against: severe real-income losses, unrest, austerity risks deepening the downturn; success still depends on tourism recovery and debt sustainability. Judgement: harsh but probably necessary — design (transfers, pacing) is what separates this from failed SAPs. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain the roles of the IMF, the World Bank and the WTO in the world economy. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the view that IMF conditionality does more harm than good to borrowing countries. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) IMF: stability, surveillance, crisis lending. World Bank: long-term development finance and knowledge. WTO: trade rules and dispute settlement. Bretton Woods origins.

(b) Harm: pro-cyclical austerity (Greece's depression; IMF's own multiplier mea culpa), poverty impacts, sovereignty, one-size-fits-all, moral hazard. Good: ends unsustainable positions, restores market access, anchors reform; counterfactual is disorderly default; modern programmes add social floors. Judgement: conditionality per se is defensible — its design and pacing decide the balance; case evidence required.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain why a developing country experiencing a balance-of-payments crisis might turn to the IMF rather than commercial lenders. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether structural adjustment programmes have promoted development in low-income countries. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Markets closed/prohibitive in crisis; IMF lends when no one else will, at lower rates; its seal of approval catalyses other finance; technical support.

(b) For: macro stabilisation achieved (hyperinflations ended, deficits closed); some liberalisations raised efficiency. Against: 1980s–90s Africa/Latin America — growth disappointing, health/education spending cut, poverty up short-run; institutions ignored. Modern view: stability necessary but insufficient; ownership, governance and human capital (SDG era) drive development. Judgement: mixed record, improved design.
